title: Introduction to Computer Systems
summary: What is a computer, the Von Neumann architecture, information representation, and historical overview.
content_html:
What is a Computer?

A computer is a device that processes information — it represents, manipulates, and stores data using binary signals (0s and 1s).

Binary Information

  • Bit: Binary digit — the fundamental unit of information (0 or 1).
  • Byte: 8 bits — the smallest addressable unit in most computers.
  • Word: The natural size of data processed by the CPU (8, 16, 32, or 64 bits depending on architecture).

Why Binary?

Binary is used because electronic circuits naturally represent two states (high/low voltage). It is simple, robust against noise, and universal laws (Shannon-Hartley) make it optimal for digital transmission.

Von Neumann Architecture

The standard computer design proposed by John Von Neumann (1945) consists of:

  • CPU (Central Processing Unit): Contains ALU (arithmetic/logic) and Control Unit.
  • Memory: Stores both instructions and data.
  • Input/Output: Interfaces for external devices.
  • Bus: Communication channel connecting all components.
Memory ←→ CPU (Control Unit + ALU) ←→ I/O
        ↑
       Bus connects all

Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle

1. Fetch: Read next instruction from memory (address in PC)
2. Decode: Interpret the instruction opcode
3. Execute: Perform the operation
4. Increment PC and repeat

Historical Timeline

  • 1940s: ENIAC, von Neumann architecture (vacuum tubes)
  • 1950s–60s: Transistors, batch processing
  • 1970s: Microprocessors (Intel 4004), personal computers
  • 1980s–90s: PCs, networking, Internet
  • 2000s+: Multi-core, GPUs, cloud computing, embedded systems

Moore's Law

Transistor count on chips doubles approximately every 18–24 months, leading to exponential growth in computing power. This trend has slowed significantly since ~2015 due to physical limits.
